findings.summary: infrastructure/publishing/core.py is a pure re-export shim with no own logic except two thin functions
evidence: Lines 24-47 of publishing/core.py: every import carries '# noqa: F401' because nothing in the module uses those symbols — they exist only to be re-exported through __init__.py
publishing/__init__.py then re-imports everything from .core, creating a two-hop indirection: caller -> __init__ -> core -> citations/metadata/models/platforms
The only real logic in core.py is create_publication_package() and create_submission_checklist(); all other public symbols are forwarded from sibling modules
This means a change to infrastructure/publishing/citations.py forces readers to trace through core.py as an intermediary, blurring the actual boundary
findings.suggestion: Eliminate core.py as an intermediary: move create_publication_package() and create_submission_checklist() directly into the most relevant sibling module (metadata.py or a new package.py), and have __init__.py import directly from citations, metadata, models, and platforms. This removes one unnecessary indirection layer.

findings.summary: infrastructure/llm/review/generator.py imports directly from infrastructure/validation/, creating an unexpected cross-subsystem dependency
evidence: Line 61 of generator.py: 'from infrastructure.validation.pdf_validator import extract_text_from_pdf, PDFValidationError'
The LLM subsystem should receive pre-extracted text from its callers rather than reaching into the validation subsystem to do PDF extraction itself
This creates a direction violation: llm/ (a higher-level consumer layer) directly depends on validation/ (a peer utility layer) rather than receiving text through a well-defined seam
findings.suggestion: Move extract_text_from_pdf out of the call path inside generator.py: callers of the LLM review pipeline should extract text first and pass a string. This would sever the llm->validation import and respect the subsystem boundary.

findings.summary: infrastructure/core/exceptions.py performs OS introspection (platform.system, shutil.which) at exception instantiation time inside __init__
evidence: Lines 422-453 of exceptions.py: MissingDependencyError.__init__ executes 'import platform', 'import shutil', and shutil.which() inside __init__ to generate recovery commands
Exception constructors with I/O-level side effects (filesystem/process calls) are unusual in an exceptions module and create surprising coupling to the runtime OS state
This makes the exceptions module implicitly dependent on the host OS at instantiation time, not just at import time
findings.suggestion: Pre-compute recovery commands lazily or move the OS detection logic to a helper in infrastructure/core/environment.py, passing the result as a parameter to the exception. Keep exceptions.py free of I/O calls.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/validate_markdown_cli.py re-implements find_markdown_files, collect_symbols, and validate_images locally instead of delegating to the canonical markdown_validator module
evidence: Lines 51-78: find_markdown_files() is implemented from scratch using os.listdir and manual sorting, separate from the infrastructure.validation.markdown_validator.find_markdown_files function
Lines 81-105: collect_symbols() and lines 108+: validate_images() are also re-implemented locally, duplicating logic that exists in markdown_validator.py
The file comment says 'All business logic is in infrastructure/markdown_validator.py' but then proceeds to implement its own business logic below
Other CLI files in the same directory (validate_pdf_cli.py) do not follow this pattern — they import from the validator module
findings.suggestion: Remove the local re-implementations and import from infrastructure.validation.markdown_validator. The thin-orchestrator pattern is already stated as the goal but not followed here.

findings.summary: infrastructure/validation/doc_scanner.py uses deferred function-level imports for all doc_discovery functions rather than top-level imports, unlike every sibling module in validation/
evidence: Lines 57-92 of doc_scanner.py: each private helper method (_find_markdown_files, _catalog_agents_readme, _find_config_files, _find_script_files, _analyze_documentation_file, _extract_headings) defers its import inside the method body
audit_orchestrator.py and all other validation sibling modules import at the top of the file normally
The deferred pattern here is applied uniformly within the class but inconsistently with the rest of the package
findings.suggestion: Move the deferred imports to the module top-level, matching the convention of sibling modules. If circular imports were the original motivation, restructure the affected modules rather than hiding the coupling with function-level imports.

findings.summary: Error signaling is inconsistent across infrastructure: some functions return bool/None on failure, others raise custom exceptions, with no clear layer-based rule
evidence: file_operations.py: clean_output_directory() catches OSError and returns False on failure — no exception raised
environment.py: check_python_version(), check_dependencies(), check_build_tools() all return bool tuples; errors are logged but never raised
rendering/core.py: render_all() raises TemplateError on failure — consistent with the exception hierarchy

integrity.py: verify_file_integrity() catches Exception, logs, and returns False in the dict; verify_cross_references() catches Exception and returns all-False dict — neither raises nor signals failure distinctly

A caller of file_operations.clean_output_directory() must check a bool return value, while a caller of rendering/core.render_all() must handle an exception — both are infrastructure-layer functions with no documented contract distinction
findings.suggestion: Establish a clear rule: functions at infrastructure boundaries that encounter unrecoverable failures should raise the appropriate TemplateError subclass. Functions that are expected to partially succeed (batch validators) may return structured results. Document this contract in the module docstrings.

findings.summary: Multiple modules catch bare Exception and log without re-raising, silently swallowing errors that callers cannot observe
evidence: integrity.py line 77-79: catches Exception on file hash calculation, logs error, sets dict value to False — caller receives a False dict entry with no way to know an exception occurred vs a real hash mismatch
integrity.py line 192-194: catches Exception on data file validation, logs warning, sets consistency flag False — breaks early but swallows the exception type

rendering/core.py lines 81-98: catches Exception on Beamer slide render failure, then inside the except block calls self.slides_renderer.render() again (line 87), creating a second render attempt that is itself wrapped in a bare try/except pass — duplicate rendering attempt on error is hidden
doc_discovery.py line 239-240: catches Exception on file analysis and only logs at ERROR level, returning no indication to caller
findings.suggestion: For batch validators, collect errors into a structured result type (list of ErrorDetail objects) rather than converting them to False booleans. For rendering, remove the redundant re-render attempt inside the except block. Ensure callers can distinguish 'check ran and failed' from 'check could not run'.

findings.summary: infrastructure/rendering/__init__.get_render_manager() is a one-line wrapper that adds no policy, translation, or behavior over RenderManager(config)
evidence: Lines 22-31 of rendering/__init__.py: get_render_manager(config) does exactly 'return RenderManager(config)' — the function body is a single return statement with no added logic
This is exactly the pattern described as a defect: a function that only forwards args to another function without policy or translation
RenderManager.__init__ already accepts Optional[RenderingConfig] and handles the None case itself (line 27 of core.py: 'self.config = config or RenderingConfig.from_env()')
findings.suggestion: Remove get_render_manager() and export RenderManager directly. Callers can instantiate it with or without a config. If a factory is ever needed for dependency injection or environment wiring, add logic at that point.

findings.summary: The codebase maintains two parallel error-description systems (InfraError constants in errors.py and exception classes in exceptions.py) with no clear rule for when to use each
evidence: errors.py defines InfraError frozen dataclasses (PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED, STAGE_FAILED, SCRIPT_EXECUTION_FAILED, etc.) used for formatted log messages
exceptions.py defines a full exception hierarchy (PipelineError, ScriptExecutionError, BuildError, etc.) covering the same domain

pipeline.py imports from both: 'from infrastructure.core.errors import PIPELINE_STAGE_FAILED, SCRIPT_EXECUTION_FAILED, STAGE_EXCEPTION, STAGE_FAILED' AND raises exceptions from the hierarchy elsewhere
There is no documented rule distinguishing when to use InfraError.format() for logging vs when to raise a PipelineError — the abstraction boundary is implicit and inconsistent across callers
findings.suggestion: Document the intended division: InfraError constants are for human-readable log output only; exception classes are for control flow. Ensure the distinction is written in the module docstrings of both files so future contributors know which to reach for.
